title: Space Tech Briefing — June 11, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space enthusiasts! It's Thursday, June 11th, 2026, and you're locked into the Space Tech Morning Briefing, your daily dose of cosmic innovation and exploration! We've got a fantastic lineup today, revisiting some monumental steps in human spaceflight and environmental protection from orbit.

First up, as we await today's new launches, let's look back at a major recent development in lunar exploration. Just a few days ago, on June 9th, NASA made a groundbreaking announcement! The crew for the upcoming Artemis III mission has been revealed. And guess what? Europe is heading back to the Moon! ESA's very own Italian astronaut, Luca Parmitano, has been selected to join three American astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft. This is a crucial intermediate mission before Artemis IV targets a lunar landing in 2028. Artemis III itself will be a vital two-week flight in low Earth orbit, designed to rigorously test the docking procedures with the future Human Landing System lunar landers. A monumental step, bringing us closer to humanity's return to the lunar surface!

And now, shifting gears from deep space to our home planet, let's highlight an incredible initiative making waves in environmental science. The Space4Ocean Alliance, after celebrating its one-year anniversary, is pushing the boundaries of satellite technology to protect our precious oceans and coastlines. This powerful consortium is dedicated to deploying decisive tools, harnessing the unparalleled view from space to confront the severe impact of climate change on our marine ecosystems. From monitoring sea levels to tracking pollution and coastal erosion, Space4Ocean is proving just how indispensable satellites are in safeguarding our planet for generations to come.

What an exciting time to be alive, witnessing humanity reach for the stars while simultaneously using the power of space to protect our home. That's all for your Space Tech Morning Briefing today, June 11th, 2026. Keep looking up, keep exploring, and we'll catch you tomorrow for more cutting-edge space news. Have a stellar day!
